data.title: IXN: Global Tech Leadership Remains, Eyeing A New Record High
data.description:
Global technology stocks petered out last October. Their high was well before equities more broadly reached their peak in January and February. Indeed, the iShares Global Tech ETF (IXN) remains below last year's zenith, but significant headway has been made just in the past two weeks.

Coming off the All-Country World Index’s best two-week rally going back to November 2020, the thrust is being led by some of the downturn's weakest areas, like IXN. I had a buy rating on the ETF back in July of 2025. Shares are up 17% since then, and I reiterate a buy rating today. I’ll provide a refreshed look at the fundamentals, valuation, seasonality, and technical situation.

IXN: Alpha to the World of Stocks YoY, Sharp Comeback Lately

According to the issuer, IXN offers investors exposure to electronics, computer software and hardware, and information technology companies and seeks to provide access to technology stocks from around the world. It’s primarily an Information Technology sector ETF, but it also holds holdings from other geographies outside the US, which helps from a diversification perspective.

IXN is a growing ETF, now with $6.8 billion in assets under management as of April 10, 2026. That’s up from just $4.3 billion at the time of my previous analysis. Its annual expense ratio is moderate at 39 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is modest at only 1.01%--significantly below that of the ACWI.

Share-price momentum has been strong lately, despite the aforementioned stagnant price action back to the early part of the fourth quarter of last year. With an A ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, I’ll note key levels on the chart to monitor later in the article. As it stands, the product can be risky at times, evidenced by elevated volatility. The current implied volatility has retreated to 36%, implying a daily swing of more than 2%.

Finally, in terms of liquidity, IXN sports decent metrics. Average daily volume is near 300,000 shares, while its median 30-day bid/ask spread is somewhat tight at 8 basis points.

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star, Bronze-rated ETF by Morningstar plots along the upper row of the style box, indicating its bent to global large caps. Importantly, from a diversification point of view, there’s 25% allocated to international equities, which is a good thing when ex-US beats domestic shares. More than half of the fund is invested in large-cap growth, so the secular story of AI penetration is a key driver of IXN’s alpha.

Right now, the price-to-earnings ratio is very reasonable at under 18x. Recall last July, it was above 27x. Paired with a long-term earnings growth rate of 19.1%, the resulting PEG ratio is below 1, which is a solid GARP bargain, in my view.

IXN: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

The 100% Information Technology ETF is highly concentrated, adding to its risk profile. Between NVIDIA (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL), that’s more than a quarter of the portfolio right there. Microsoft (MSFT), the only Mag 7 stock to be negative over the past 12 months, is also a hefty position at more than 9%.

Beyond that, diversification is decent. In all, the top 10 stocks account for an elevated 57% of the fund. Be on guard for potential volatility later this month when mega-cap tech begins reporting Q1 results.

IXN: Holdings & Dividend Information

From a seasonal perspective, we are right ahead of what has been a very bullish period. The May through August stretch has been IXN’s best when scanning price return data over the past 10 years.

Add this to the bullish-indicator camp.

IXN: Bullish Calendar Stretch Ahead

The Technical Take

With a compelling valuation, alpha over the past year, and strong seasonals, IXN’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that shares broke down below their long-term 200-day moving average but then sprouted right back above it. Now, the ETF is back to the breakdown point from earlier this year. I’d like to see another thrust toward the all-time high of $113 from last October on stronger momentum. The 60 RSI level is indeed not quite as high as I’d like.

Also take a look at where long-term support lies—just under $90. If we take the measured move upside price objective based on the mid-2024 to April 2025 decline and subsequent breakout, $114 is the target—the October 2025 high. So, a correction down to $95 was certainly reasonable. Recall that I calculated that $114 level last July, so everything has been playing out as the technicals would script.

Today, with a bullish false breakdown in play, I expect new highs later this year. The bulls will have to crack through a somewhat high amount of volume by price up to the $110 level. If so, it should be clear skies above.

IXN: Technical Target Hit, Pullback Appears Complete, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on IXN. The ETF has done all the right things, technically. The valuation, meanwhile, is downright compelling, with a PEG ratio under 1 heading into a bullish calendar period.

data.title: HDV: Defensive ETF That Protects Capital But Limits Returns
data.description:
Overview

The positive market momentum over the last decade may have spoiled investors. Growing wealth in the market was as easy as throwing money into a technology-focused fund and remaining patient. But what if capital rotated out of technology and the sector stopped growing so rapidly? With the sell-off of the software sector, we get to see firsthand how rotation of capital out of technology is a possibility. When growth becomes more difficult because of sentiment or macroeconomic factors, this is where the iShares Core High Dividend ETF (HDV) may be valuable. When I previously covered HDV, I released an educational article that covered all aspects of the fund. With the uncertainty of the markets, I wanted to revisit the fund to reassess its value proposition going forward.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that HDV's share price has increased by more than 18.8%. The ETF continues to demonstrate its ability to participate in positive market growth, despite the heightened uncertainty. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up above 24% over the same time frame. HDV offers a small starting dividend yield of 2.9% that's paid out on a quarterly basis. While the starting yield is low, the utility of HDV is in the dividend growth that can occur over a longer holding period.

HDV's main benefit is its ability to provide resilience and protection during periods of market uncertainty. The focus on high-quality dividend-paying companies means that HDV can provide a sustainable source of income during declines, which can be ideal for investors that care about capital preservation. Unfortunately, the same thing that makes HDV a defensive investment is the same thing that makes HDV an underperformer over a long holding period. The fund lacks any meaningful allocation to growth industries, which limits returns through bull markets.

Fund Strategy

When I was starting my dividend investing journey, I started to slowly accumulate shares of all the different dividend aristocrats and kings out there. However, this required a lot of upfront capital to be invested in order to obtain shares of all the companies I wanted. Without even realizing it, I was manually building my own diversified ETF. However, the ETF eliminates all the guesswork and makes it very easy to get instant exposure to all the highest-quality dividend growth companies out there.

According to the latest fund overview, HDV has total net assets of $13.6B that are spread across a diverse range of dividend-paying equities. The ETF is very cost-efficient with a management fee of 0.08% of assets. HDV is most heavily allocated to consumer staples at 23.97% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to energy and health care companies, which accounts for 22.15% and 17.57% of assets, respectively. These top three sectors make up more than 63% of assets, so investors should anticipate that these will have the greatest impact on the fund's daily price movement.

When selecting its positions, HDV filters for specific eligibility requirements. For instance, the company must have a projected forward dividend yield that is higher than the market average. HDV also tends to focus on companies that have moats and are considered leaders in their respective industries. Lastly, the ETF only considers positions that are the least likely to experience any sort of financial hardship, which is achieved by avoiding companies that are heavily leveraged.

When looking at the top holdings for the fund, we can see that HDV provides direct exposure to some of the highest-quality dividend-paying companies in the world. Admittedly, the fund does lean a bit top-heavy towards its top ten holdings. The top ten positions make up more than 52% of total assets, so these will have the greatest impact on HDV's daily price movement.

However, I associate these top ten positions with the highest quality of dividend-paying companies. For instance, Exxon Mobil (XOM) is currently the largest position at 9.34% of total assets. This is followed by other notable companies, including Chevron (CVX), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), AbbVie (ABBV), Procter & Gamble (PG), and Philip Morris International (PM). Just to help conceptualize the sort of companies that HDV focuses on, I wanted to highlight some of the dividend strengths for its holdings:

  • Merck & Co. (MRK): maintains a very healthy dividend payout ratio of 36.12%.
  • Progressive (PGR): achieved an annual dividend growth rate of 23.19% over the last five years.
  • Coca-Cola Company (KO): 63 years of consecutive dividend raises without interruption.

Defensive Positioning

The indices have been off to a rough start for the year as capital rotates out of technology and into other areas of the market. The market is pricing in threats of AI as the capabilities and cost-efficiency of different LLMs are such versatile tools for businesses of all scales. AI is improving operating efficiency in terms of cost, speed of execution, and a reduction in headcount. Therefore, investors are concerned that the moat software companies once had is now being eliminated.

On top of that, the market is uncertain about the massive level of capex spending for the large-cap leaders. As the demand for AI grows, companies have allocated capital towards building out the infrastructure needed. However, investors are questioning how and when these investments will translate into a higher base earnings growth. So while index-tracking ETFs have traded sideways over the last few months, HDV has been able to benefit from this rotation out of technology and into more value-oriented holdings.

On a YTD basis, HDV's total return now sits above 12%, which outpaces the return of the indices. For instance, the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) is now trading near breakeven, while the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) is also down by 0.5% over the same time frame. By focusing on companies with a healthy level of free cash flows, these businesses tend to be more stable over time.

When backtesting HDV's performance through prior market declines, it is clear that the fund's appeal is in its ability to preserve capital. For instance, the markets declined through 2022 in response to rapid interest rate hikes and uncertainty surrounding the pandemic. As a result, SPY experienced a total loss of 18.17%, while QQQ saw a total loss of 32.58% for the year. In comparison, HDV was able to provide a positive total return of 7.06% over the same time frame.

This is all to say that HDV's appeal is the ability to offset a prolonged market decline. This is why HDV can be a great option if you are cautious about the geopolitical conflict in the Middle East getting worse. We also have rising unemployment, high inflation, and uncertainty around AI that can drive the U.S. indices down. Therefore, HDV can be a great defensive position within a diversified portfolio of assets. The ability to protect capital ultimately comes down to the fund's low allocation to technology companies, which can limit volatility.

Dividend Utility

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.8430 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 2.9%. While the starting dividend yield isn't that impressive, it still remains more than double what you can expect from a traditional S&P 500 ETF. However, the point of holding HDV is for the dividend safety and growth over time. I would argue that HDV's main objective isn't necessarily to provide dividend growth, like a peer fund such as the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). Instead, HDV's portfolio construction is built for sustainability and reliability, which is still valuable for investors that want a low-stress dividend position.

Despite the low yield and mild dividend growth, HDV still has a history of slightly raising payouts over time. For instance, HDV was able to increase its dividends at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 3.38% over the last ten years. So if you were an investor who initiated a position exactly ten years ago, you would now have a yield on cost above 5%. The growth rate can certainly be better. Once again, HDV's emphasis is on safety and sustainability.

To demonstrate the growth potential of HDV, I ran a backtest of an initial $100,000 investment at the start of 2017. This back test assumes that no additional capital was ever invested after the initial $100k, but it also assumes that all dividends received were reinvested back into the accumulation of shares. By simply remaining invested, the amount of annual income investors receive would have nearly doubled over the last few years.

2017 Dividend Income: $3,633

2025 Dividend Income: $6,481

Since the yield is so small and the growth rate is mild, HDV is best utilized for retirees that have already accumulated hundreds of thousands of capital. The larger the position, the larger the income stream that can be collected from your position. So while the fund's dividend growth may not be the best, it can still be utilized for a low-stress income stream within a diversified portfolio.

Risks

The irony of HDV's portfolio is that the same thing that makes HDV a great defensive choice is the same thing that makes HDV likely to underperform market indices over the long term. At the end of 2025, I released an article titled 'A Warning To Young Dividend Investors.' I highlighted that a focus on dividends can limit total returns, since a focus on dividends tends to cause investors to miss out on growth industries. The same issue is relevant here with HDV when assessing its long-term performance. While the market is rotating capital out of technology holdings, this may be short-lived.

Ultimately, I am very optimistic about the outlook of technology, and HDV just isn't aligned to participate in the growth of AI. For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI market can expand to a value of ~$3.5T by 2033. While HDV does have some slight exposure to technology companies, the fund excludes businesses that do not pay out dividends. Therefore, a position in HDV means that you are not getting exposure to companies like Meta Platforms (META), Alphabet (GOOG), and Amazon (AMZN). Even though META and GOOG now pay dividends, they do not have an established history strong enough to be eligible for inclusion in HDV's holdings. Although these companies are likely to grow their dividends over time, their yields are extremely low, so inclusion in HDV's holdings is unlikely.

Furthermore, the decision to focus on defensive dividend-paying companies means that you are likely to underperform. HDV is reducing risk by limiting growth exposure, but it is also reducing the ability to grow capital over time. For instance, HDV's total return over the last ten years sits around 145.4%, which severely underperforms against SPY and QQQ over the same time frame.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that HDV can be an efficient long-term holding for investors that want to preserve and protect capital over a long holding period. The ETF provides instant diversification into the highest-quality dividend-paying companies that have a long track record of stability. So while HDV's focus may not specifically be on providing dividend growth, it can provide a sustainable source of income for very low-risk investors that want to avoid the volatility of technology. The issue is that the defensive positioning means that investors are likely to underperform over the long term. Therefore, HDV makes the most sense for older investors that are in the retirement phase of their life and care about protecting capital above all.

data.title: US Foods Holding: A Truly Defensive Winner Of The Trade-Down Economy
data.description:
Introduction

US Foods Holding (USFD) has been able to develop significantly in recent years, capitalizing on the ongoing macro weakness to boost their private label brands to their food distribution network, with significant M&A activity.

USFD is rated Buy, being in a strong position to take advantage of the ongoing macro weakness and uncertainty and still having a massive growth pipeline of over 100 M&A targets, proving their defensive nature - albeit the company is not immune to a recession.

Growing From the Market's Weakness

USFD reported a solid Q4 and 2025 as a whole, beating the market's EPS expectations despite a miss on revenue, with a 4.1% increase in net sales and a strong 11% boost to their Adj. EBITDA, as well as a very strong 26.3% growth in the Adj. Diluted EPS and 30 bps in Adj. EBITDA margin expansion, marking a strong start to their 2025-2027 targets, while reducing their net leverage to 2.7x.

Meanwhile, despite the increase in CAPEX, the company's FCF grew to $959 million in 2025 compared to $833 million in 2024 and $831 million back in 2023, backed especially by their M&A activity and the macro pressure seen in the industry, which I'll detail in a bit.

For 2026 specifically, USFD sees 4% to 6% growth in net sales, 9% to 13% in Adj. EBITDA, and 18% to 24% in Adj. Diluted EPS, expecting $400 million to $440 million in CAPEX this year (vs. $410 million in 2025), although that was before the Supreme Court's decision regarding tariffs, so we could see them deliver even better numbers.

The company's guidance continues their strong track record seen in recent years, marked by a ~5% CAGR in sales, ~14% in Adj. EBITDA, 23% in Adj. Diluted EPS and an excellent 1,000 bps increase in Adj. ROIC from 2022 to 2025.

Although it may seem counterintuitive at first, the current pressure on the environment made the company's foodservice clients go through trade-downs similar to how consumers did towards the retailers' private label brands in an effort to cut costs, which USFD was able to capture through their own private label/exclusive brands (which is also visible in their higher profitability per case), with the CEO highlighting their performance in this segment during the recent CAGNY Conference:

Our customers win because these are great products that they can buy at a lower cost and the company makes more money. That's why you see our exclusive brand portfolio now represents about 35% of the company's revenue. And importantly, in areas like independent restaurants, we've penetrated our exclusive brands at a much higher rate. In fact, we just covered last week that we were 54% penetrated with independent restaurants, a record level for us in the fourth quarter of last year.

This was accentuated by their significant M&A activity, allowing them to build a very powerful portfolio of private label brands that can generate solid amounts of data about their industry, which can then allow them to further capture the client through adapted price ranges and products. For example, if a client that wanted to cut costs during the current hard times eventually wants to move back up in quality, they can suggest their higher-quality Chef's Line brand or introduce higher price points for premium lines to the other existing brands.

Not only that, but this is far from over, as the company highlighted during the conference that they "have a robust pipeline of more than 100 targets, representing $15 billion," which can allow them to strengthen this position significantly and transform the business thanks especially to the broader macro trends that were a major headwind for many.

Financially, based on USFD's latest report, we can see another advantage thanks to their solid position overall, with current assets covering their current liabilities, and despite the fact that they don't have a lot of cash and assets (mostly goodwill), we should keep in mind that the business is not asset-heavy by design, so the equity will always be far from the market cap.

Especially following the failed attempt to merge with Performance Food Group (PFGC) back in late 2025, the company's focus returned to their aggressive buybacks, returning $926 million in FY25, for a yield of roughly 4.6% today, although they announced a new $1 billion authorization in November and have nearly $1.1 billion left now.

USFD is expected to report earnings on May 8, with the market expecting $0.81 in EPS and $9.64 billion in revenue. Although I don't expect some major swings, I can see them adjusting their guidance to the new tariff environment, and I believe the company can offer very important insights into the broader industry or even the economy as a whole, potentially indicating continued trade-downs from the industry or even the consumer, which can mean issues for many other companies that depend on the consumer's discretionary budgets. Especially with the Iran conflict still being a developing matter, I would expect an overall cautious tone regarding the environment.

Iran Pressure Can Still Hurt Them

Despite the company's performance, it's important to note that they are not immune to the environment, and just because they were able to capitalize on the trade-downs made by restaurants in their effort to reduce costs in the face of tariffs and an overall weak consumer environment even before that, it doesn't mean things can't get worse.

Even if we see an immediate ceasefire and the Strait of Hormuz traffic restarts, a wave of inflation may still hit the economy, as we're talking about much more than just higher pump prices.

This inflationary pressure can spread into virtually everything, from the higher fuel or fertilizer prices we see now into higher transport fees, more expansive crops (or weaker yields), etc., which can put the Fed in a position where it may have to even consider hiking rates or at the very least delaying the expected cuts, which then translates into pressure on the economy and the stock market.

Meanwhile, USFD's clients can face higher borrowing costs, more restaurant closures, weaker discretionary budgets, and less demand, which can ultimately pressure the company's sales or margins.

Not only that, but we could see even more significant pressure for foodservice in general, as people can move away even from the value-oriented restaurants into cooking at home, with low-cost retailers' private label brands being the ultimate winners.

I'll also mention the GLP-1 adoption since I notice the market's high interest in it and the potential impact it can have on food consumption in general.

Although there isn't enough data to be able to properly estimate its impact on the foodservice segment, we saw some studies indicating its impacts on grocery spending, while a recent report from Morgan Stanley estimates GLP-1 adoption at about 15% of the US population by 2035, which may sound like a major hit, but it's likely much smaller than the market fears, while USFD and its clients can adapt to health-consciousness trends through different menu additions (e.g., lower-calorie, high-protein, etc.), helping them offset the potential hit.

Sure, 15% is a massive adoption rate for the medicine producers, and it's a major tailwind for them, but the headwind for the general food/restaurant/retail/etc. industries doesn't seem like such a big concern, with price increases likely being able to offset it.

Meanwhile, a recovery or even a higher-for-longer rate environment can be good for USFD, allowing them to continue capitalizing on potential trade-downs while building their portfolio to capture the upside in a recovery through more acquisitions.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, although the company can benefit from certain levels of economic weakness, and they were able to do it thanks to the smart moves made by the management, there's also a limit to how bad the environment can get before affecting them.

Especially if the Iran conflict lasts for longer than the market expects, there is certainly the possibility of seeing it push the global economy into a recession, to which not even this company is immune.

Valuation

For USFD's DCF to Equity valuation, starting from last year's $959 million in FCF and assuming no changes in net borrowings, I'll assume a 20% CAGR until 2027 based on their EPS guidance (also taking into account the effect of buybacks on the number of shares), followed by a 12% CAGR until 2030 and 8% until 2035, as the business can continue benefitting from their impressive growth pipeline, synergies, and private label margin expansion, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a 10% discount rate to take into account the macro risks, as well as 225.1 million diluted shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $26.46 billion, for an intrinsic value of $117.55 per share, which is still above the current $91.42 at the moment of writing this, indicating that the market is perhaps even more careful, demanding a better margin of safety.

Conclusion

USFD is rated Buy, backed by a strong business that's been able to prove its ability to capitalize on a weak environment and develop significantly, standing to benefit from more weakness, albeit not immune.

There are risks to remember, as the environment can rapidly deteriorate into one that would imply less eating away from home if the inflationary shock caused by the ongoing Iran conflict leads to a recession, but the overall risk-reward seems to be in the company's favour, proving to be a proper defensive stock during times of weakness, while most of the stocks considered defensive in the food industry are struggling.

data.title: BIZD: Private Credit Is Still In Trouble
data.description:
It's the end of the world as we know it…and I feel fine

-R.E.M.

I was walking past the office of Cliffwater in Marina Del Rey last week. For the asset manager who brought private credit to the masses, it's looking more and more like the end of the world as we know it.

Today, we revisit the VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD), the largest exchange-traded fund that invests in business development companies. Let's review prior coverage and update on systemic challenges facing the industry.

Review of Prior Coverage

BIZD is my fund of choice for covering the business development company sector. It continues to be the largest and most liquid business development company-focused exchange-traded fund. In past coverage, I have discussed the management fee structure, including a base management fee of less than 100 basis points. The fund offers a high dividend yield and pays a quarterly dividend that currently amounts to 14% on a trailing 12-month basis.

My prior coverage of the fund was around six months ago. In the Fall, there were some small cracks that were beginning to show in the private credit industry. This included the bankruptcy of two companies. My prior coverage dove into current events and discussed how pocketed bankruptcies were considered business as usual in the business development company space.

The reality of credit investing is that things move in slow motion. Our perception of reality moves much faster than reality itself causing our perceptions to go from very high to very low very quickly. In the case of a major bankruptcy like First Brands and Tricolor, investors are quick to flee viewing them as a cockroach in the kitchen. The idea that these two bankruptcies could be the first of many has begun to increase investor scrutiny towards the private lending and private credit worlds.

In reality, the impact of these two bankruptcies is much less significant than our minds are making us believe. Keep in mind that the loans issued by these companies receive enormous spreads because of the sub-investment-grade credit profile of the borrower.

A large piece of the discussion was emphasizing investor psychology. As a relatively young industry, private credit has seen relatively few challenges. Many investors were looking at these early bankruptcies and wondering if they represented the canary in the coal mine for more upcoming problems that could face the industry.

At the time, business development companies were beginning to fall as early investors took chips off the table ahead of what many saw to be trouble in paradise. Over the past six months, the picture has changed significantly, with the troubles facing business development companies moving far beyond pocketed bankruptcies that mostly affected collateralized loan obligations that held the debt.

Now, it appears as though the rising non-accrual rate over the past year has begun to catch up with business development companies, and more have begun to hint towards dividend adjustments.

Let's talk about more changes.

That's Great, It Starts With an Earthquake

Private credit was the belle of the ball for the greater part of the past decade. Leveraged loans, business development companies, and everything else under the private credit umbrella saw a renaissance period.

The past several years led to an unprecedented growth period for the private lending industry. Even on the heels of the massive growth after the financial crisis, the post-pandemic era has seen a supercharged private lending environment. The world of private credit was historically limited to institutional investors who were able to stomach the illiquidity. Companies like Cliffwater sought to change this dynamic. By offering private credit to retail investors through interval funds and a corresponding network of financial advisors, Cliffwater has gone from a small independent research firm to a billion-dollar asset manager.

While billionaire founder and chief executive Stephen Nesbitt, alongside his CIO and thirty-something-year-old son Blake, continues to take charge leading the battle, the tides appear to be turning against the fast-growing industry. Under pressure, the Nesbitt duo has been intransigent, even going so far as to compare themselves to Jack Bogle, the founder of Vanguard, who received skepticism for his belief in index funds. This is certainly a bold way to describe yourself, especially when your investors are at your front doors asking for their money back.

"It is no surprise the Wall Street establishment does not want this product to succeed," Blake Nesbitt wrote on social media last week. "We will wear it as a badge of honor instead of a Scarlett [sic] Letter, as we aspire to one day be welcomed into Mr. Bogle's fine company," Blake Nesbitt wrote.

Only time will tell if the billionaire and son will emerge as the next Buffett or Bogle, but in the meantime, their flagship fund is seeing their largest redemption request in the company's history. This appears to be continuing to spread wider and wider, going beyond illiquid private credit funds and business development companies. More funds are gating redemptions, and right now, publicly traded business development companies are getting caught up in the mix.

Birds and Snakes, and Aeroplanes

Now, it appears as though no stone has been left unturned. Asset managers, business development companies, and CLO equity have taken the hit head-on, and their performance has been difficult over the past six months.

What began as a series of pocketed bankruptcies within specialized borrowers has quickly spread to systemic trouble that is facing most market participants. Outside of what we are seeing with the equity position of collateralized loan obligations, there are a variety of different troubling factors that are facing the industry.

Let's start with something at the highest level. Redemption requests across private credit funds have been massive through the first quarter. According to the Financial Times, investors requested to withdraw an unprecedented $20 billion from private credit funds during the first quarter. This activated gating across funds from managers such as Blue Owl (OWL), Apollo Global Management (APO), Ares Management (ARES), and Cliffwater.

The $20.8 billion in redemption requests hit huge groups in the sector, including Apollo Global Management, Ares management, Blackstone, Blue Owl and KKR, according to FT calculations.

The funds tracked by the FT, which collectively management assessment portfolios worth about $300 billion, have honored just over half of the redemption requests they receive. Many investors have been forced to wait until it redemption window opens up later this quarter to exit.

According to the Financial Times, investors sought to collect around 7% of their investment across the entire industry. It's easy to see the public asset managers have been punished as a result.

If we were to look beyond what many investors are predicting as the next financial crisis, the redemptions also have a significantly negative impact on the management fees collected by many of these private credit funds. $21 billion in redemptions corresponds to hundreds of millions of dollars a year in lost fees, assuming this capital does not come back quickly. With the value of the underlying assets further deteriorating, it creates another headwind that causes the management fees collected by the asset manager to decline.

Then let's go even further and talk about the cross-investment issue that's facing some of these managers. Cliffwater, with their interval funds, was able to bring the private credit industry to the masses. This looks an awful lot like packaging illiquid investments into a semi-liquid vehicle where the manager could incorporate a buffer component, in this case publicly traded business development companies, that can help manage the illiquidity challenges. Now, what we are seeing is that the redemptions facing the Cliffwater "Turducken" are likely to put significant pressure on other asset managers since Cliffwater will begin trying to redeem shares in funds that they hold.

Among the issues that are facing borrowers, I see this cross-investment issue as something considerable for these funds. While there might not necessarily be an extreme bankruptcy wave, significant shrinkage of the industry could cause a consolidation issue that punishes a fund of funds.

And Lenny Bruce is Not Afraid

There is also momentum moving in the other direction. Recently, ARES announced they are planning a new private credit vehicle with a fundraising goal of $20 billion. This would likely help relieve liquidity pressures for other business development companies, as the new fund would be able to buy assets out of funds facing elevated redemption requests.

This is reminiscent of the impact on REITs during the interest rate squeeze. Capital quickly becomes a battle of the haves and the have-nots. In that case, Blackstone (BX) has massive non-traded REITs that were facing record redemption requests, forcing them to gate redemptions (sounds familiar?). This allowed other market participants with liquidity to step in and purchase assets at prices they found attractive.

Still sticking to the BX example, Rexford Industrial (REXR) stepped up and bought $1 billion of properties covering three million square feet, closing in early 2024. This could transition to new private credit funds alleviating the pain for ongoing BDC redemption requests.

It's the End of the World as We Know It…and I Feel Fine

So, what do you do in a time like this? From my perspective, tough times in private credit look a lot like tough times in any other sector. The best time to get out was a year ago, but that doesn't help us looking forward. Instead, we should reassess our options.

BIZD does a good job of offering investors exposure to an entire sector in a convenient package. The fund is relatively cheap and requires no rebalancing on the part of the investor. This makes it an easy option to make a thematic play on publicly traded business development companies.

I still maintain that those looking for business development company exposure may be better served by choosing an actively managed exchange-traded fund, such as the Putnam BDC Income ETF (PBDC). Looking at this fund specifically, it continues to outperform BIZD over most time frames.

Another opportunity is to look at higher-quality business development companies. Right now, my recommendation continues to be low-leverage business development companies with little exposure in the software sector. Most recently, this came in the form of Gladstone Capital (GLAD), one of the oldest public business development companies. I see the low leverage profile, conservative group of borrowers, and discount to net asset value as attractive.

I also believe BDCs with a higher equity component have a chance to outperform. Fan favorites like Main Street Capital (MAIN) have been top performers in recent years, but their expanded valuation scares me too much for an investment. Instead, some may consider looking into other equity BDCs, such as Gladstone Investment (GAIN), which trades at more reasonable valuations with a similar business model.

Over the past year, GAIN has considerably outperformed MAIN.

Conclusion

I look at private credit as being an industry in need of consolidation. Following nearly two decades of extreme growth, the private lending industry is facing challenges. Like any industry, a challenging period will separate the strong from the weak and show you who has been swimming naked. We have already seen this take shape with some of the weaker, higher-leveraged business development companies taking it on the chin. That said, the industry has been negatively affected across most participants. This means that there are likely diamonds hiding in the rough, and at this point, I don't believe taking an indexed approach is necessarily the best option.

While BIZD holds merit as being one of the most convenient ways for diversified exposure to business development companies, I see other options as offering more merit at this time. BIZD earns a Hold rating.

data.title: Mastercard: Finding Reasons For The Selloff (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Mastercard (MA) shares are down 13% YTD, continuing a five-year trend of underperformance.

This selloff comes at a time when fundamentals have shown no weakness whatsoever, leaving investors searching for an explanation.

We'll make an attempt at finding the reasons for the selloff, but, spoiler alert, I'm upgrading Mastercard to 'Buy'.

I downgraded the stock back in October, claiming the growth gap had almost fully closed, while Mastercard's premium valuation remained. However, as fundamentals accelerate and the stock goes in the opposite direction, that setup is no longer true. In fact, I find Mastercard to be underestimated, trading at the cheapest valuation in years.

A Surprising Stretch of Underperformance

Investors got used to seeing Mastercard's stock working like clockwork. In essence, all you had to do was hold for the long term, and this high-quality compounder did the rest of the work.

Well, this hasn't really worked over the past five years, with Mastercard vastly underperforming the market.

Fundamentals Are Not the Reason

One thing is clear: fundamentals are not the reason for the lagging.

Over the past five years, Mastercard grew revenues at a 16.5% CAGR, with growth accelerating in 2025. Operating margins expanded by over four points, resulting in a 19% operating income CAGR. With the addition of buybacks, EPS grew an impressive 21% annually.

It's quite rare to see a stock rising less than 8% annually during a period when earnings have grown almost triple that pace.

And no, the trend hasn't changed in recent quarters, so it's not like we're relying on past performance. Mastercard is still very much delivering the same level of results.

Finding Reasons for the Selloff

I'm sure most readers and Mastercard investors already knew that fundamentals are very strong and came here looking for fresh ideas regarding the poor performance. So, let's get to that now.

In my view, there are four potential explanations for Mastercard's lagging, ranking them by order of importance: Business Mix; Starting Valuation; Visa's Acceleration; Disruption Concerns, and; Regulation.

Let's tackle these, one by one.

Business Mix Leaning Toward Value-Added Services

In 2020, 65% of Mastercard's revenues came from the legacy payment network business. This is Mastercard's decades-old proven moat, effectively an oligopoly alongside Visa, which is considered by many to be one of the best businesses in history.

As of Q4-25, this part of the business dropped to 56% of sales. While it continues to deliver strong double-digit growth, Value-Added Services are becoming the dominant source of growth and are likely to surpass the Payment Network in business mix by the end of this decade.

The Value-Added Services business line includes many verticals, but the key thing to understand is that it's not at the same business quality as the Payment Network business. That's at least how the market views it.

So, if Payments Network deserves a 30x earnings multiple, VAS probably deserves 20x-25x, which justifies some multiple contraction over time.

Starting Valuation

Entering this five-year underperformance stretch, Mastercard traded at quite a lofty valuation. The graph shows over 60 times trailing earnings, and on a forward basis, it was somewhere in the vicinity of 40x.

So, obviously, expectations were quite high, and it could be argued that we are just in the process of a long recalibration to a more reasonable level.

Visa's Acceleration

For several years, I've been saying here on Seeking Alpha that Mastercard's huge premium over Visa (V) wasn't sustainable. Simply, the justifications investors gave for said premium lacked important nuance. With the gap in revenue growth between the two narrowing, so have their respective valuations.

Indeed, Mastercard's five-year average premium of 20% has now contracted to less than 8% today:

Disruption Concerns

Those who have been following the payment giants for quite some time know that the market is taking turns with which is the latest candidate to be the one to disrupt the payment networks.

There was FedNow, Crypto, QR Payments, and now, of course, AI and Stablecoins. Surely, trillions of dollars of payments done on their rails will transition to some "Agentic Stablecoin Monster".

Of course, I'm being sarcastic. But it's a very real concern for the markets.

Regulation

As always, the regulatory boogeyman is always hovering above Mastercard's and Visa's heads. The more recent concerns are about President Trump's proposal to limit credit card rates and the EU wanting to decrease its dependency on Visa & Mastercard.

Why I'm Not Concerned, Rating Upgrade

At $499 per share, I find that most, if not all, of the investor concerns are diminished relative to the opportunity.

Mastercard now trades at 25 times forward earnings, which translates into ~1.5x PEG, one of the lowest ratios I've seen for a high-quality company like Mastercard.

Further, this valuation reflects, roughly, a 30x multiple for the payments business and a 20x multiple for Value-Added Services. To me, both are fair, with some downside risk for the payments side, but far higher upside risk to the VAS side.

The gap from Visa is almost non-existent, and disruption and regulatory headwinds are, as Mastercard's history taught us, temporary.

I estimate that the stock couldn't continue its disconnect from fundamentals for much longer, and there's a long gap to close.

Conclusion

Mastercard is in the midst of a surprising five-year underperformance stretch. It doesn't have much to do with fundamentals but rather with a valuation reset and a temporary spike in negative sentiment.

Looking ahead, I expect the stock to return to moving alongside fundamentals, which means at least ~15% annual returns, aligned with earnings growth.

As such, I am upgrading Mastercard to 'Buy.'

data.title: CrowdStrike: A Wing And A Prayer
data.description:
The cybersecurity space took a wild spin last week after the release of Project Glasswing by Anthropic (ANTHRO). CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD) originally jumped on the news before eventually succumbing to the fears AI will replace traditional cybersecurity. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock due to valuation, though AI is still likely poised to boost cybersecurity demand.

Threat or Foe

Anthropic claims Claude Mythos is so powerful the AI model requires the company to work directly with 50 different large companies to ensure the safety of the global economy. Anthropic is working with CrowdStrike and Palo Alto Networks (PANW) to ensure cybersecurity tools aren't overwhelmed by the AI model.

The Claude Mythos Preview was so successful in finding unknown software vulnerabilities, Anthropic created Project Glasswing to work with large companies responsible for digital infrastructure. The fear is that Clause Mythos Preview could open up hackers to exploit vulnerabilities in previously undiscovered code errors.

CrowdStrike initially soared due to the stock market viewing the company as having a competitive advantage over other cybersecurity vendors due to working directly with Anthropic on this project. The market changed tunes quickly, seeing Claude Mythos as a direct threat to cybersecurity technology, fearing the worse.

For its part, CrowdStrike announced a $500 million increase in the share buyback plan to a total authorized amount of $1.5 billion. The company has already spent $150 million buying 413K shares at $364.57 per share.

The CFO even went so far as to reconfirm an ARR target as follows:

We have increased our share repurchase authorization to $1.5 billion to opportunistically return value to shareholders as we progress toward our goal of reaching $20 billion in ending ARR by FY36.

Just prior, Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora bought 68,085 shares at $146.87 per share for $10 million. These moves by the leading cybersecurity companies signal the whole industry isn't buying into the AI threat.

Claude Mythos offers an AI model capable of finding code flaws and will be utilized as part of a platform to help improve cybersecurity performance. Though the AI model hasn't been released to the public yet, any timeline is unknown.

An interesting attempt to use Claude to pick stock investments ended up with the AI model buying ServiceNow (NOW). This enterprise workflow company was crushed another 8% on Friday due to fears over Claude replacing the SaaS business.

The irony of everyone fearing an AI model replacing platform applications, yet the model sees the platform as an incredible buy on the dip due to Claude being the default model powering the ServiceNow Build Agent platform. ServiceNow utilizes Anthropic to enhance the data workflow since the AI model doesn't provide enterprises with a viable platform without the guardrails, cloud connections, and data integrations of ServiceNow.

CrowdStrike is a similar scenario for cybersecurity. The company will, in theory, work with Claude Mythos to enhance their enterprise cybersecurity solutions as the delivery mechanism, not a victim.

UBS sees the AI threat as boosting cybersecurity spending in the short term. As well, AI models have shown the ability to inadvertently unleash attacks, with the senior fellow at Georgetown University's Center for Security and Emerging Technology suggesting more AI producing more code just opens up new vulnerabilities, whether or not a model like Claude Mythos finds issues in old code.

Paying the Right Price

The issue with the stock for a long time has been the extreme valuation. CrowdStrike has fallen below $380 again, with a recent low of nearly $343.

Even after falling over 30% from the 52-week highs, the stock still trades at 16x FY27 revenue targets of $5.9 billion. The company is only forecast to grow revenues at a 20% clip over the next few years.

If anything, CrowdStrike is vastly overpaying for the stock here with the share buybacks. The stock trades at nearly 80x EPS targets while only growing sales at a 20% clip.

If AI is a threat, CrowdStrike would trade at close to $100 with a 20x multiple on a nearly $5 EPS target for FY27. If AI is a foe, the stock shouldn't be valued any higher, with the company needing 30% to upwards of 40% growth to warrant even the current price.

The risk is the stock trading much lower under a real AI threat from Anthropic. The reward is the stock trading at this level and relying on an irrational market to pay a higher price, such as the Benchmark target of $500.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that all the data continues to suggest AI models like Claude Mythos need a delivery mechanism like cybersecurity platforms from CrowdStrike. The company is likely to see a higher demand for cybersecurity spending to protect against AI threats. The problem is that the stock isn't priced for the AI risk, offering limited legitimate upside.

data.title: Geopolitical Shock Tests Moat Strategies As Energy Surges
data.description:
U.S. equities fell in March as oil surged on geopolitical tensions. The Moat Index lagged on no energy exposure, while the SMID Moat Index held up with help from energy and materials.

Index performance is not illustrative of fund performance. It is not possible to invest directly in an index. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Fair value estimates and price targets referenced herein are those of Morningstar's equity research team, are subject to change without notice, and do not constitute recommendations or investment advice.

U.S. equity markets suffered a sharp, broad-based decline in March as the escalation of the U.S.-Iran conflict upended what had been a constructive start to the year. The initiation of U.S. and Israeli military strikes on Iran in late February and Iran’s subsequent disruption of oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz sent energy prices surging. Brent crude rose above $100 per barrel during the month. The S&P 500 fell 4.98% in March, while the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index declined 5.97%, reflecting the pervasive nature of the selloff across market capitalizations and styles. Energy was the only positive sector during the month, gaining 10.28%, while industrials, consumer staples, and health care were among the hardest hit, each falling more than 8%. A late-month rally on hopes for a potential ceasefire helped indexes recover from their worst levels, but was not enough to offset a punishing month for equities.

The Morningstar Wide Moat Focus Index (MOAT) (the “Moat Index”) declined 9.55% in March, trailing the S&P 500 by roughly 4.5 percentage points. The Index reached its deepest drawdown around March 27, when cumulative month-to-date losses approached 12%, before a late-month recovery trimmed the gap modestly. Both sector allocation and stock selection weighed on relative performance. The strategy carries no allocation to energy, a reflection of the wide moat requirement for index inclusion, as commodity-oriented businesses rarely develop the durable competitive advantages that Morningstar looks for in assigning wide moat ratings. That absence proved especially costly in March, as energy was the month’s only positive sector. Sizable overweights to industrials and consumer staples, two of the worst-performing sectors, compounded the shortfall. For the first quarter, the Moat Index declined 6.49%, trailing the S&P 500’s 4.33% loss. Even the NASDAQ Composite fell nearly 7%, illustrating how broadly the geopolitical shock weighed on equities regardless of market capitalization or style.

The Morningstar US Small-Mid Cap Moat Focus Index (SMOT) (the “SMID Moat Index”) fell 5.40% in March, essentially matching the S&P MidCap 400’s 5.39% decline while trailing the S&P SmallCap 600, which lost 4.07%. Small- and mid-cap stocks broadly declined alongside large-caps during the month, as the geopolitical uncertainty drove a correlated selloff that offered limited diversification benefit across capitalizations. Attribution was roughly neutral between allocation and selection. The strategy’s energy exposure, a byproduct of the SMID Moat Index’s inclusion of narrow moat companies that broadens the investable universe into sectors like energy where wide moats are scarce, providing a meaningful offset to weakness in consumer discretionary and consumer staples holdings. Materials holdings, led by CF Industries, were the standout positive contributor to relative performance. Through the first quarter, the SMID Moat Index and smaller-cap companies more broadly have fared better than large-cap benchmarks, as the diversified nature of small- and mid-cap exposure has provided a degree of resilience in an uncertain environment.

Geopolitical Shock Pressures Equities in March

Moat Index Leans Into Tech Opportunities at Quarterly Review

Both the Moat and SMID Moat Indexes underwent quarterly reviews on March 20, 2026. Each quarter, Morningstar’s equity research analysts systematically target the most attractively priced, high quality U.S. companies within their respective universes. At the March review, the Moat strategies capitalized on technology dislocations driven by AI uncertainty and geopolitical volatility, adding semiconductor leaders NVIDIA and Broadcom alongside newcomers Palo Alto Networks, Blackstone, and Datadog at attractive valuations. See our blog covering the recent review for additional context and key takeaways. Full results of the quarterly reviews are also available here: Moat Index and SMID Moat Index.

Moat Index Highlights: Cybersecurity Strength in a Difficult Month

March was a challenging month for the Moat Index from a relative performance standpoint, with both sector allocation and stock selection contributing to the shortfall versus the S&P 500. The strategy’s zero exposure to the surging energy sector was a notable headwind, while overweights to industrials and consumer staples, two sectors that bore the brunt of war-related economic concerns, amplified the underperformance. Against this difficult backdrop, the top contributors were concentrated among technology names that managed to buck the broader decline.

Fortinet Inc. (FTNT) and Palo Alto Networks Inc. (PANW) were the leading contributors to Moat Index performance during the month, with both names posting modest gains while the broader technology sector declined. Both are platform-based cybersecurity vendors whose shares held up well amid the market’s risk-off posture. Cybersecurity stocks experienced a brief selloff late in March following reports of a new AI model with advanced vulnerability-finding capabilities. However, Morningstar views the development as likely to expand the cybersecurity addressable market rather than diminish it, as more capable AI tools drive demand for both offensive and defensive security solutions. Morningstar assigns wide moat ratings to both Fortinet and Palo Alto, with Fortinet’s competitive position underpinned by customer switching costs and a reinforcing network effect derived from its expansive installed base, and Palo Alto’s wide moat supported by its entrenched position as a platform vendor with strong customer switching costs across network security, cloud security, and security operations. Both companies trade meaningfully below Morningstar’s fair value estimates.

Other notable contributors during the month included Blackstone Inc. (BX), an alternative asset manager that was added to the Index during the quarterly March reconstitution, and Fair Isaac Corp. (FICO), an analytics and decision management company.

Companies detracting the most from Moat Index performance reflected the broad-based nature of the month’s selloff. The Estee Lauder Companies Inc. (EL), a prestige beauty company, was the largest detractor, with shares falling roughly 34% amid ongoing operational challenges. Huntington Ingalls Industries Inc. (HII), a defense and shipbuilding company; Clorox Co. (CLX), a consumer products company; United Parcel Service Inc. (UPS), a global logistics provider; and Otis Worldwide Corp. (OTIS), an elevator and escalator manufacturer, also weighed meaningfully on results. The detractors were spread across consumer staples and industrials, the two sectors most heavily overweighted in the portfolio, reflecting the outsized impact those sector tilts had during a month when defensive and cyclical names alike were caught in the downdraft.

Source: Morningstar. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. Index performance is not illustrative of fund performance. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or to sell any of the securities mentioned herein.

SMID Moat Index Highlights: Energy and Materials Offset Broad Weakness

The SMID Moat Index navigated the turbulent March environment more effectively on a relative basis, with allocation and selection effects roughly neutral versus benchmarks. The strategy’s exposure to energy and materials provided a meaningful counterweight to weakness elsewhere, helping the Index keep pace with the S&P MidCap 400 despite the challenging backdrop.

CF Industries Holdings Inc. (CF) was the standout contributor, with shares surging approximately 30% during the month. As the largest nitrogen fertilizer producer in North America, CF Industries is a direct beneficiary of the supply disruption caused by the U.S.-Iran conflict, which has curtailed Middle Eastern nitrogen exports and driven fertilizer prices sharply higher. Morningstar raised its fair value estimate for CF to $135 per share in mid-March, citing expectations that the conflict will support elevated nitrogen prices in the near term. Morningstar assigns CF a narrow moat rating based on the company’s cost-advantaged position, as over 90% of its nitrogen production uses low-cost North American natural gas as feedstock, placing it well below the marginal cost of global production.

Devon Energy Corp. (DVN) and EOG Resources Inc. (EOG) also contributed meaningfully, with each gaining roughly 16% during the month as rising oil prices lifted the domestic exploration and production sector broadly. Both companies are positioned at the low end of the U.S. shale cost curve, with Devon’s reconstituted portfolio anchored in the Delaware Basin and EOG’s multibasin approach emphasizing its highest-return drilling locations. Morningstar assigns both companies narrow moat ratings based on cost advantages derived from access to premier acreage with intrinsically low extraction costs.

Other notable contributors included Akamai Technologies Inc. (AKAM), a content delivery and cybersecurity company, and Marvell Technology Inc. (MRVL), a semiconductor firm.

Several of the SMID Moat Index’s largest detractors mirrored those seen in the Moat Index. The Estee Lauder Companies Inc. (EL) and Huntington Ingalls Industries Inc. (HII) were again among the weakest performers, while Carnival Corp. (CCL), a cruise line operator that suffered from rising fuel costs and travel disruption fears, also detracted. Otis Worldwide Corp. (OTIS) and GE HealthCare Technologies Inc. (GEHC), a medical technology company, rounded out the bottom five.

data.title: Are There Still Opportunities In Europe And Asia Despite High Oil Prices?
data.description:
Despite ongoing pressure from high energy costs tied to the Middle East conflict, select sectors in Europe and Asia are showing resilience. Michael Brown, Vice President, Director and Portfolio Manager with TD Asset Management, breaks down where investors can find potential growth and navigate risks in an increasingly volatile landscape.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Higher oil prices are proving headwinds to economies in Europe and Asia, but our feature guest today says there are still some global sectors which can thrive in this environment. Joining us now to discuss is Michael Brown, VP, Director, and Portfolio Manager with TD Asset Management. Michael, great to have you back on the program.

Michael Brown: Thank you for having me.

Greg Bonnell: It has been an interesting, obviously, four or five weeks, the soaring oil prices definitely taking their toll on global economies in different ways. Let's start with Europe amid higher energy prices. How are you seeing the market?

Michael Brown: Okay, it's a very interesting environment with quite a bit of increased volatility. I mean, as you know, Europe is an energy importer and dependent on quite a bit of oil energy from the Middle East, particularly with no Russian oil and gas coming -- or less Russian oil and gas coming in. So the higher energy prices is a headwind to their economy. It increases the risk of stagflation.

You've seen this weighing on some of the more cyclical sectors, more energy-dependent sectors. Some banks have been weak, as well as some travel-related businesses, like the airlines and commercial aerospace. However, what you have also seen is some pretty strong performance in the energy names.

Now, typically, in times of volatility, we choose to look for more enduring trades versus some of these short-term fluctuation trades. So I mean, you can make a strategy of buying energy when it's low, selling when it's high. Going back and forth over a period of days is not our strategy. We choose to look for enduring trades where there's actually an extended period of potential outperformance.

Many of these names have been doing quite well, both fundamentally and as stocks going into the conflict, and have continued to do so through the conflict and, we believe, going further out, so.

Greg Bonnell: Okay, let's start breaking down some of the areas that you touched on there briefly. I want to start with, perhaps, the opportunity and utilities and industrials in Europe.

Michael Brown: So utilities is a key area that we do like in Europe. I mean, it's a question of, in Europe, renewable energy, improving their energy independence. It's not a question of just a green initiative. It is a matter of national defense, which has been exacerbated by this conflict in Iran. It has shown how much Europe needs to move towards independent energy. Now, that could be renewables, nuclear, and/or importing LNG.

So all of these are quite attractive areas, whether it be gas distribution from LNG terminals -- which NG would be a company that's a leader there -- or improving the grid, which could be several industrials feeding into that. One would be ABB (ABBNY) or Siemens Energy (SMEGF). There's also, in terms of LNG, there's a company called Gaztransport (GZPZF). And the name is actually French, Gaztransport, which would do -- they have an exclusive technology for the membranes for LNG, cryogenic freezing, and transportation.

I mean, all of these are benefiting on a secular trend of moving towards reducing energy dependence in Europe. So they were doing well previously, continue to do well. I mean, obviously, the key caveat on these would be the valuation. These are not secrets. So it is watching to make sure that you still have a positive potential return on the stock.

Greg Bonnell: I imagine part of that play, too, no matter how you're generating your electricity, is to strengthen the grid that transmits that. We know in North America, we have an old grid. What's the situation in Europe?

Michael Brown: I'd say it's a similar, underinvested area. They are actively investing in it. Many of these, whether it be construction, engineering, industrial companies, like Hochtief (HOCFF) or Prysmian (PRYMF) that does the cables, are all part of this expansion in the grid improvements. Obviously, the AI data center boom is contributing to the needs here. But it is -- overall, an improved power grid is positive for the economy of Europe, as well as their energy sustainability, so.

Greg Bonnell: You mentioned travel and tourism. These stocks have really been battered around over the past several weeks, given what's happening in the Middle East. What's the opportunity here?

Michael Brown: I think, looking at this, there's been a pretty dramatic sell-off, particularly in almost blanket selling on airlines, as well as hotels, as well as commercial aerospace. I think where we'll be seeing the opportunity would be largely within the commercial aerospace space. As long as the conflict does not go on for an extended period of time, flight hours would continue benefiting some of the aftermarket that are dependent on flight hours.

In addition, it's also important to look at where the actual exposures are of these travel-related names. Are they exposed to the Middle East? Are they exposed to North America? Are they exposed to Continental Europe? The higher energy prices, if they stay longer for a longer period of time, could negatively impact travel, or it could change the way people travel. People still go on vacation. It just might be to more local, driving holidays, that sort of thing, so. It's still looking --

Greg Bonnell: That's Europe. Let's talk about Japan, because we know that when it comes to the energy crunch that we've seen over the last several weeks, Asian countries are particularly very susceptible to it, very sensitive. What's Japan look like?

Michael Brown: So first, with Japan, they do have amongst the highest energy inventories in the world. So they have been through this before. They're prepared for this. So short term, there is a cushion. Obviously, the longer energy prices stay higher as it moves along the futures curve, they do become more vulnerable to that.

They do, in terms of power generation, have a lot of nuclear capacity. They also, following Fukushima, have a lot of coal capacity as well. So the power generation is there. It might just be at a higher price or may accelerate the restart of their nuclear generation there.

I mean, in terms of Japan, there's still many drivers beyond the energy costs. Some of that would be in terms of the revitalization with Japan, like the construction engineering. We're also seeing in terms of the AI data center trade that is continuing, whether it be semi-cap equipment or even some of the materials, looking at real asset companies and some of the metals supply chain that come through, or the big five trading houses in Japan, which would be major conduits in terms of international trade, bringing in metals requirements for Japan.

Greg Bonnell: What are the hopes, too, for that economy in terms of some reforms, some stimulus, also helping and supporting the market?

Michael Brown: As we talked about last time when I was on here, the current parliament under Takaichi is the largest majority in post-war Japan. So they do have the ability to push through fiscal legislation. They do have the ability to control policy, working with the BOJ in concert in terms of managing interest rates, as well as paying close attention to the yen, where yen stability would be key as terms of the rate hikes. There is worries on the yen there. I think that is the greatest risk facing Japan.

But really, domestic stimulus, what you're seeing, continued capital reforms that have been going on for a number of years where concentration on expanding the profitability of businesses, returning capital to shareholders, and really becoming more shareholder friendly as a market overall.
